SUMMARY:
Frontiers | The view of synthetic biology in the field of ethics: a thematic systematic review The view of synthetic biology in the field of ethics: a thematic systematic review The study’s main question is defined as, _“What are the main ethical debates in the field of synthetic biology, and what are the contents of these ethical debates?”_ The publications that met the criteria selected by the systematic review method were included in the analysis, and the relationship between synthetic biology and ethics was examined inductively. Keywords: synthetic biology, ethics, bioethics, systematic review, technology ethics, responsible research and innovation Citation: Kurtoğlu A, Yıldız A and Arda B (2024) The view of synthetic biology in the field of ethics: a thematic systematic review.

SYSTEMATIC REVIEW article

Front. Bioeng. Biotechnol., 28 May 2024

Sec. Synthetic Biology

Volume 12 - 2024 | [https://doi.org/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796](https://doi.org/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796)

The view of synthetic biology in the field of ethics: a thematic systematic review
==================================================================================

[![Image 5: Aye Kurtolu ](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2674966/74)Ayşe Kurtoğlu](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/2674966)*[![Image 6: Abdullah Y&#x;ld&#x;z](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1971408/74)Abdullah Yıldız](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/1971408)![Image 7: Berna Arda](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)Berna Arda

*   Department of Medical History and Ethics, Ankara University School of Medicine, Ankara, Türkiye

Synthetic biology is designing and creating biological tools and systems for useful purposes. It uses knowledge from biology, such as biotechnology, molecular biology, biophysics, biochemistry, bioinformatics, and other disciplines, such as engineering, mathematics, computer science, and electrical engineering. It is recognized as both a branch of science and technology. The scope of synthetic biology ranges from modifying existing organisms to gain new properties to creating a living organism from non-living components. Synthetic biology has many applications in important fields such as energy, chemistry, medicine, environment, agriculture, national security, and nanotechnology. The development of synthetic biology also raises ethical and social debates. This article aims to identify the place of ethics in synthetic biology. In this context, the theoretical ethical debates on synthetic biology from the 2000s to 2020, when the development of synthetic biology was relatively faster, were analyzed using the systematic review method. Based on the results of the analysis, the main ethical problems related to the field, problems that are likely to arise, and suggestions for solutions to these problems are included. The data collection phase of the study included a literature review conducted according to protocols, including planning, screening, selection and evaluation. The analysis and synthesis process was carried out in the next stage, and the main themes related to synthetic biology and ethics were identified. Searches were conducted in Web of Science, Scopus, PhilPapers and MEDLINE databases. Theoretical research articles and reviews published in peer-reviewed journals until the end of 2020 were included in the study. The language of publications was English. According to preliminary data, 1,453 publications were retrieved from the four databases. Considering the inclusion and exclusion criteria, 58 publications were analyzed in the study. Ethical debates on synthetic biology have been conducted on various issues. In this context, the ethical debates in this article were examined under five themes: the moral status of synthetic biology products, synthetic biology and the meaning of life, synthetic biology and metaphors, synthetic biology and knowledge, and expectations, concerns, and problem solving: risk _versus_ caution.

1 Introduction
--------------

Synthetic biology is a branch of science that combines knowledge from biological fields such as molecular biology, biophysics, biochemistry, medicine, biomedical engineering, biotechnology, bioinformatics, and systems biology with disciplines such as engineering, physics, chemistry, mathematics, computer science, electrical engineering, and mechanical engineering ([McDaniel and Weiss, 2005](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B48); [Lam et al., 2009](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B42); [Cheng and Lu, 2012](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B10); [Keshava et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B40)). This field studies the feasibility of creating organisms or parts using living and non-living materials. As a branch of engineering, synthetic biology endeavors to design and produce these organisms and their parts, utilizing these materials ([Häyry, 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B26)).

Synthetic, meaning manufactured, often contrasts with natural. However, in the context of biology, it refers to the combination of two or more biological parts, either by design or by natural processes. Synthetic biology involves designing and creating biological tools and systems using engineering principles. While this definition has similarities with modern biotechnology, synthetic biology is distinguished by its emphasis on terms such as design, creation, construction, device, and system ([Porcar and Peretó, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B59)).

Synthetic biology is expected to have broader applicability than biotechnology in molecular biology and engineering. While biotechnology mainly focuses on using controlled biological circuits to design and produce new products, synthetic biology opens new avenues. It provides new opportunities to use artificial biological circuits to understand fundamental biological problems ([Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B58)).

The successful synthetic design of _Mycoplasma mycoides_ has turned theoretical concepts into reality, demonstrating that a newly engineered life form or cell can survive and reproduce ([Gibson et al., 2010](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B19)). This achievement is particularly intriguing because, despite its synthetic origin, the cell is still governed by its genes, which are also synthetic. In essence, a synthetic cell has been brought to life, representing the creation of a new species. Such a groundbreaking scientific development inevitably requires ethical evaluation, given the potential positive and negative implications for the future.

Synthetic biology represents one of the most significant recent examples of the necessity for ethical considerations in the face of the unexpected becoming expected or possible. It has prompted the emergence of several crucial questions across a range of contexts. On the one hand, there are questions regarding the extent of responsibility researchers should assume and the degree of freedom they should enjoy in their work. Additionally, it prompts us to consider the limits of open science and how to protect scientific publications and results. Furthermore, the government’s stance on the public interest and the industry’s role in these discussions must be considered. Finally, the challenge lies in balancing the dilemma of benefit and harm. Given the state of bioethics in the modern era and its close relationship with technology, synthetic biology may represent a new turning point in bioethics ([Kaebnick and Murray, 2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B37)).

Ethics, especially bioethics, must be highly responsive to advances in their respective fields. However, due to the dynamic nature of the field, there are inevitably gaps in how ethics responds to these issues. Although gaps between practice and ethics are inevitable, the size of these gaps often paves the way for the emergence of undesirable problems. This leads to distrust of scientific disciplines and research or those conducting them. It is important to take a proactive approach to ethics and reduce the gap as much as possible ([Askland, 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B2)). In order to reduce this gap, ethical awareness on the subject and the establishment of codes, especially for practice, should be established. For this purpose, it is essential to raise the awareness of the scientific world and society on ethical issues ([Rappert, 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B66)).

This study aims to trace the emergence of ethics in synthetic biology over time. In this context, we systematically analyzed the theoretical ethical debates on synthetic biology from the 2000s to 2020, which coincides with a relatively faster and newer period in the development of synthetic biology. This research aims to present and discuss the basic framework of the theoretical debates on the ethics of synthetic biology for both medical and life scientists and ethicists.

2 Materials and methods
-----------------------

The study’s main question is defined as, _“What are the main ethical debates in the field of synthetic biology, and what are the contents of these ethical debates?”_ The publications that met the criteria selected by the systematic review method were included in the analysis, and the relationship between synthetic biology and ethics was examined inductively. Based on the results, the main ethical issues related to the field, the problems that are likely to arise, and suggestions for solutions to these problems are given.

The data collection phase of our research involved a systematic literature review that included planning, screening, selection, and evaluation, all of which were conducted according to established protocols. We then undertook an analysis and synthesis process to identify key themes related to synthetic biology and ethics. We used the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) checklist for the screening and analysis phases. However, due to the specific nature of our study, not all items on the checklist were applicable ([Moher et al., 2009](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2024.1397796/full#B52)).

A literature search was conducted after determining the scope of the search and keywords. Databases were selected to provide search results in medicine, biotechnology, engineering, law, ethics, and bioethics. The literature search was conducted in the Web of Science, Scopus, MEDLINE Complete, and PhilPapers databases. Three of these databases were included because they are relatively the most comprehensive databases to provide an overview of the debate. PhilPapers was used to include the philosophical aspect of the scientific debate.

Abstract
--------

[Synthetic biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/synthetic-biology "Learn more about Synthetic biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an emerging multidisciplinary field that aims to design and construct new biological systems not found in nature. Whereas synthetic biology may yield tremendous benefits, it may also pose substantial risks to human health and the environment that must be addressed. In this paper, we examined the environmental risks associated with synthetic biology, including changes to or depletion of the environment, competition with native species, horizontal gene transfer, [pathogenicity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pathogenicity "Learn more about pathogenicity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or toxicity, bioterrorism, and laboratory [biosecurity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biosecurity "Learn more about biosecurity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). We highlight three approaches for assessing environmental risks in synthetic biology: solution-focused risk assessment, Bayesian networks, and network of networks for sustainable capacity building. An ethical governance framework is proposed to facilitate innovation while minimising risks. This framework emphasises the precautionary principle and balancing stakeholder interests prior to project development and commercialisation. Overall, we underscore the importance and urgency of assessing and managing the environmental risks of synthetic biology to ensure its safe and ethical development and application.

1. Introduction
---------------

Synthetic biology, a multidisciplinary subject that emerged at the beginning of this century, is etymologically derived from the French term “biologie synthétique”, which was coined by French physical chemist Stéphane Leduc in 1912[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0005). Synthetic biology uses systems biology methodology to profoundly modify or design new biological systems[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0010). The emergence of synthetic biology has revolutionised biological research ideas and methods, providing humanity with a new way to understand the concept of “life” and transforming the relationship between humankind and the natural world. Unlike traditional biology, which adopts a “top-down” approach wherein the object of study is first grasped as a whole and then gradually reduced and analysed by deconstructing its parts and understanding the relationships between them[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0015), synthetic biology uses a “bottom-up” approach that involves assembling biomolecules and parts in a “building-to-understand” way to comprehend the essence of life and the whole of nature.[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0020), [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0025)

Since its inception, synthetic biology has garnered worldwide attention from the scientific community and governments. In 2000, one paper focused on “toggle switch”[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0030) and another focused on “oscillatory network”[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0035) were published in _Nature_, considered seminal works in synthetic biology. In 2004, the MIT Technology Review listed synthetic biology as one of the 10 emerging technologies that would change the world[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0040). In 2006, the US National Science Foundation took the lead in funding the establishment of the Synthetic Biology Research Center (SYNBERC),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0045) and in 2007, the European Commission launched 18 synthetic biology research projects[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0050). In 2014, the United States (US) Department of Defense announced its six “disruptive technologies” for the next 5 years, including synthetic biology.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0055) The Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) in the US has placed particular emphasis on synthetic biology research, releasing multiple related projects such as Advanced Plant Technologies and Insect Allies, with projects established by all three military branches [12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0060). In 2018, the European Strategy Forum on Research Infrastructure (ESFRI) released a roadmap proposing the innovative distributed infrastructure “Industrial Biotechnology Innovation and Synthetic Biology Accelerator” (IBISBA), aimed at supporting industrial biotechnology and synthetic biology to promote Europe’s transition toward a circular bioeconomy.[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0065) In July 2021, the United Kingdom (UK) released its “Strategy for UK Life Science”, which indicated that the UK had invested approximately £45 million in the field of synthetic biology and hoped to establish an independent department to develop a related technology roadmap, providing recommendations for actions necessary to establish a world-leading synthetic biology industry.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0070) In July 2022, the UK’s national centre for the commercialisation of synthetic biology (SynbiCITE) received funding from the UK government to strengthen and support synthetic biology companies in the country, enhancing the potential of the UK bioeconomy and unlocking the [social benefits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/social-benefit "Learn more about social benefits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") brought about by synthetic biology.[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0075)

Depending on the research scale, synthetic biology can be divided into gene circuit design, whole-genome synthesis, and creating artificial life forms[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0080). The concept of gene circuits originates from the logic circuit concept in engineering, which is a collection of coding RNAs or proteins that function within or between cells to fulfil some logical function.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0085) Gene circuits are somewhat independent and often form a degree of [orthogonality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/orthogonality "Learn more about orthogonality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with the gene network of the chassis cell. A chassis cell in synthetic biology is the host cell where metabolic reactions occur and is a virtual synthetic biology reaction platform into which the functionalised components, circuits, and pathways of synthesis are systematically placed for rational design purposes[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0090). Examples of gene circuits include the “toggle switch”, “oscillatory network”, and “bistable circuit” [19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0095), [20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0100). Whole-genome synthesis refers to the chemical synthesis of the entire gene sequence of an organism[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0105), and it has been performed for viruses[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0110), [23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0115), prokaryotic [mycoplasma](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mycoplasma "Learn more about mycoplasma from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"),[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0120) and eukaryotic yeast (near completion).[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0125), [26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0130) The aim of creating artificial life forms is no longer to replicate and modify natural life forms but to study the process of transforming matter from non-living to living; the goal is to synthesise chemical systems in vitro and give them life-giving properties such as metabolism, reproduction, genetics, and mutation.[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0080) From the current history of synthetic biology, research has mainly focused on the artificial synthesis and editing of simple life “genomes”. Designing and creating life is still a long way off, as the current technological level cannot fully synthesise the material basis required for the functioning or metabolism of life and its dependence on the environment because scientists have only synthesised the genomes of these organisms, However, their cytoplasm, cell membranes, and other components of life are still natural rather than synthetic.

According to the Research Council of the National Academy of Sciences in _Biodefense in the Age of Synthetic Biology_, synthetic biotechnology can be defined as enabling technologies developed to achieve the design–build–test cycle, which includes both technologies explicitly developed for synthetic biology and technologies that have integrated conventional biotechnology.[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0135) Synthetic biotechnology has been developed on the foundation of traditional biotechnology, particularly molecular biotechnology. It encompasses various techniques, including DNA synthesis and assembly, [recombinant DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/recombinant-dna "Learn more about recombinant DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology, machine learning and automation, directed evolution, genome editing, and cell-free synthetic biotechnology.[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0140) Among these, DNA synthesis is the underlying technology driving synthetic biology research, and chemical synthesis of DNA is currently the most widely adopted DNA synthesis method[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0145). Compared with traditional biotechnology, which modifies an organism’s genome by adding or deleting genes, synthetic biotechnology has achieved a qualitative leap forward in its ability to modify an organism’s genome. By creating and adding genes that do not exist in nature, synthetic biotechnology endows modified life forms with novel properties designed to emerge at the discretion of humans[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0150). As Polish scientist Waclaw Szybalski points out, “All along, people have been doing the descriptive side of molecular biology…but the real challenge begins when we reach the synthetic biology stage. We will design new regulatory elements, add new molecules to existing genomes, or construct a new genome.[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0155), [32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0160)″ Synthetic biotechnology is at the forefront of creating organisms with distinctive characteristics by manipulating genetic material. With the ability to introduce novel genes that do not occur in nature, scientists can engineer life forms with specific traits that humans can control. Molecular biology has focused on observing and understanding the natural world; however, with synthetic biotechnology, we can take a more active role in designing and constructing organisms from scratch. We can make new regulatory elements or construct an entirely new genome.

With the rapid development of new biotechnologies represented by synthetic biology, all sectors of society have increasingly recognised the issue of biological safety. Countries such as the US, the UK, Germany, Japan, as well as the European Union have successively enacted regulations on biosafety management[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0165). Regarding time, milestones in international biosafety management include the International Health Regulations, developed as early as the 1950s[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0170) and the Biological Weapons Convention passed in 1971[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0175). The Convention on Biological Diversity was signed and passed at the United Nations Conference on Environment and Development in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, in 1992[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0180). Its supplementary treaty, the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety to the Convention on Biological Diversity, took effect in 1993[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0185). Both defined biosafety at the level of international law. On October 7, 2020, the 22nd meeting of the Standing Committee of the 12th National People’s Congress of China passed the Biosafety Law of the People’s Republic of China, which occurred on April 15, 2021[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0190).

There is no doubt that considerable uncertainty exists in applying synthetic biology or biotechnology, which has increased concerns about the risks of this technology in environmental, ecological, social, economic, and human respects. With the emergence of new technologies, there is the potential for risks and crises. Humans have learned from countless scientific and technological accidents, including the Fukushima nuclear power plant explosion, Chernobyl, [genetically modified foods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/genetically-modified-food "Learn more about genetically modified foods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and gene-edited babies. In particular, when certain technologies involve testing on organisms and have the potential to alter genetic material, people become more vigilant about their potential risks. If risk governance and ethical frameworks are not prioritised before project development and commercial operation, public and media opinion can substantially hinder the progress of emerging synthetic biology. Moreover, the potential consequences of not prioritising risk governance and ethical frameworks can cause irreparable harm to human society and the natural ecological environment.

Therefore, in this paper, we examined the practical environmental impact of synthetic biology and synthetic biotechnology. We list several specific environmental risks associated with synthetic biology, including changes or depletion of the environment, competition with native species, horizontal gene transfer, [pathogenicity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pathogenicity "Learn more about pathogenicity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or toxicity, bioterrorism, and laboratory [biosecurity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biosecurity "Learn more about biosecurity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This analysis highlights the importance and urgency of assessing the environmental risks associated with synthetic biology. We propose three analytical approaches for assessing environmental risk in synthetic biology to achieve this goal. These include solution-focused risk assessment, Bayesian networks, and networks of networks based on sustainable capacity building. A practical governance approach should facilitate innovation, minimise risks to people and the environment, and balance the interests and values of all relevant stakeholders[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0195). In this paper, we propose an ethical framework and precautionary principles for the governance of synthetic biology. This framework is designed to minimise the potential risks of synthetic biology to human society and the natural ecological environment in advance of project development and commercial operation.

2. Relationship between environmental risk and synthetic biology
----------------------------------------------------------------

Environmental risk is a serious concern in terms of the potential negative impact of synthetic biology because both synthetic and [recombinant DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/recombinant-dna "Learn more about recombinant DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") organisms release active cells into the environment[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0200), [41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0205). Although recombinant DNA organisms have been in existence for decades, they have the potential to cause unintended effects, such as horizontal gene transfer[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0210) and unintended effects on plant physiology and ecology.[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0215) Synthetic organisms, however, can be distinguished from naturally occurring organisms in a more complex manner. It is essential to carefully consider and assess the potential environmental risks associated with all [genetically modified organisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/genetically-modified-organism "Learn more about genetically modified organisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (GMOs), including synthetic and previous recombinant DNA organisms, before their release into the environment. In their natural environment, these may persist, reproduce, spread, or even transfer their DNA to natural organisms, thereby causing damage to existing ecosystems[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0220).

### 2.1. Definitions of environmental risk

The potential environmental risks of synthetic biology can be viewed specifically in terms of biosafety and [biosecurity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biosecurity "Learn more about biosecurity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The biosafety risk of synthetic biology involves the accidental release of synthetic organisms that may alter or impact the local ecology and threaten human health throughout the research cycle of synthetic biology.[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0225) Biosafety risks can arise during the research and development (R&D) stage (i.e., escape of synthetic microbes from the laboratory and their accidental introduction into the human body or natural environment), the manufacturing stage (i.e., the possibility of harm to laboratory workers through accidental exposure to modified cells), and the obsolescence stage (i.e., improper disposal of synthetic biological by-products and waste).[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0230) In all these stages, the focus is on the possible health hazards in these unintended events. The possible consequences of unintentional release of synthetic or modified organisms (GMOs) are: (1) they may become [invasive species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/invasive-species "Learn more about invasive species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), posing a threat to biodiversity; (2) exposure to unrelated human and animal targets, causing harm; (3) modified organisms (mainly bacteria) cannot be controlled once they are taken out of their enclosures[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0235).

The biosecurity risk of synthetic biology is associated with the malicious use of synthetic biology knowledge and technology, such as in the case of bioterrorism. Because of the “dual-use” of synthetic biology, some nefarious individuals or bioterrorists may use synthetic biology principles to create biological weapons with catastrophic consequences, especially with respect to biochemical and viral–bacterial modifications that could easily lead to such consequences. In such cases, such nefarious actors use synthetic biology research in ways that deliberately cause harm to humans, animals, or the environment. The National Science Advisory Board for Biosecurity (NSABB) is a US government advisory board that provides advice and guidance on issues related to biosecurity and dual-use research. The NSABB was established in 2004 by the US Department of Health and Human Services in response to concerns about the potential misuse of scientific research and technologies for harmful purposes, such as bioterrorism[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0240). NSABB defines biosecurity as protection, control, and accountability regarding high-consequence biological agents, toxins, and critical relevant biological materials and information to prevent unauthorised possession, loss, theft, misuse, diversion, or intentional release.[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0245) Thus, the biosecurity risk of synthetic biology primarily concerns the misuse of biological agents, knowledge, or technologies.

### 2.2. Environmental impacts of synthetic biotechnology

The environmental impact of synthetic biology depends on the species designed, type of alteration, location of release, strategy of release and monitoring, and nature of the genetic alteration.

One critical determinant of the environmental impact of synthetic biology is the species being designed or modified. Some organisms may be more resilient or adaptable to environmental stressors than others whereas other organisms may be more susceptible to disease or predation. A study found that Bt cotton, which produces an insecticide to protect against pests, had a lower incidence of other [insect damage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/infestation "Learn more about insect damage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") than conventional cotton crops[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0250). Bt cotton reduces the need for pesticide use, leading to lower [environmental pollution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-pollution "Learn more about environmental pollution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and potential harm to non-target organisms. However, another study found that Bt cotton negatively affects non-target arthropod populations[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0255), leading to lower biodiversity and potential ecological consequences. During the [Zika virus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/zika-virus "Learn more about Zika virus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pandemic between 2015 and 2016, a company called Oxitec used gene drive technology (the details of which are described later) to create mosquitoes with a trait defect that would cause them to die before reaching adulthood owing to transcriptional silencing, thereby reducing the transmission rate of Zika virus via the _[Aedes aegypti](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aedes-aegypti "Learn more about Aedes aegypti from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ mosquito vector.[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588933823000201#b0260) This raises numerous concerns, such could this modification lead to _A. aegypti_ being eliminated as a species, and if so, how much will eliminating _A. aegypti_ affect the entire ecosystem?

Executive Summary
-----------------

Synthetic Biology (SynBio) has many different definitions, but can generally be defined as “an interdisciplinary field of biology and engineering that manipulates DNA for the purpose of designing and redesigning systems such as genes, biological pathways, or organisms” ([Johnson et al. 2024](https://www.bakerinstitute.org/research/what-synthetic-biology#:~:text=Synthetic%20biology%20is%20an%20interdisciplinary,problems%20and%20understand%20biological%20principles.)). Scientists and engineers are using SynBio tools to create new medical therapies, identify disease outbreaks, breakdown chemicals, and develop more sustainable fertilizers. These promises of new innovations also bring ethical challenges for researchers and questions about the use of their products.

In this paper, we highlight six major areas or themes related to ethical challenges associated with SynBio research and products: 1) engineering life; 2) risks and potential benefits; 3) promises, speculations, and hype; 4) equity and distribution of risk, benefits, and access; 5) research collaborations; and 6) environmental ethics. Each theme is defined, described and linked to associated research and publications on the topic. The goal of the paper is to provide a high-level review of major ethical issues, provide additional resources for investigators to learn more about the subject, and contribute to the overall discussion on the implications of SynBio.

**View the full paper (**[**PDF**](https://www.bakerinstitute.org/sites/default/files/2025-06/20250606-Matthews%20et%20al.-Synthetic%20Biology-Working%20Paper.pdf)**).**

enriched_points.expanded_content: Responsible innovation in synthetic biology emphasizes ethical practices, ensuring that scientific advancements align with societal values and do not compromise public safety or environmental integrity.

Synthetic biology holds transformative potential, affecting healthcare, agriculture, and environmental sustainability, necessitating careful consideration of its social implications and equitable access to benefits.

Robust regulatory frameworks are essential for overseeing synthetic biology applications, balancing innovation with safety, and addressing ethical concerns to protect public health and the environment.
